name: Marmolejo and Yeguas Reservoir Loop
name_original: Marmolejo – vuelta por Villa del Río y Embalse del Yeguas - Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar
description: Cycle 24.1 miles (38.8 km) through Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar, past Embalse del Yeguas, with moderate difficulty.
region_intro: Road cycling around Marmolejo, located in the province of Jaén, Spain, offers diverse terrain at the foot of the Sierra Morena Mountains. The region features a landscape of Mediterranean forests, olive groves, and the Guadalquivir River. Cyclists can explore routes with varied elevations, ranging from gentle gradients along former railway lines to more challenging ascents within the Sierra de Andújar Natural Park. The area provides access to natural attractions like the Martín Gonzalo Reservoir and scenic views across the Andalusian countryside. Best road cycling routes around Marmolejo The most popular road cycling route is Marmolejo and Yeguas Reservoir Loop, a 24.1 miles (38.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 41 minutes to complete. This route offers a scenic loop around the Yeguas Reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Incredible views – Cardeña Town Center loop from Marmolejo, a difficult 59.5 miles (95.8 km) path. This route provides extensive views and passes through the Cardeña town center. Local road cyclists also love the Lopera Castle – Castillo de Lopera loop from Marmolejo, a 40.2 miles (64.8 km) trail leading through the historic area around Lopera Castle, often completed in about 3 hours 2 minutes. Road cycling around Marmolejo is defined by routes through olive groves, along the Guadalquivir River, and into the foothills of the Sierra Morena Mountains. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Marmolejo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from 4 reviews. More than 500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Marmolejo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Marmolejo?
A: Road cycling around Marmolejo offers a diverse landscape. You'll find routes traversing vast olive groves, following the scenic Guadalquivir River, and ascending into the foothills of the Sierra Morena Mountains. The region includes varied elevations, from gentle gradients, particularly along former railway lines like the Vía Verde del Aceite, to more challenging climbs within the Sierra de Andújar Natural Park.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Marmolejo?
A: Yes, Marmolejo offers several road cycling routes suitable for beginners. There are 3 easy routes available, providing a gentler introduction to the region's beautiful scenery without overly demanding climbs.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see while road cycling near Marmolejo?
A: While cycling near Marmolejo, you can explore several natural features and historical landmarks. Routes often pass by the picturesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5911606" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Martín Gonzalo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and offer views of the Guadalquivir River. You might also encounter the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301171" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marmolejo Spa&lt;/a&gt;, known for its thermal waters. For those venturing further, the Sierra de Andújar Natural Park provides stunning mountain landscapes and opportunities to spot diverse wildlife.

Q: Can I cycle on the Vía Verde del Aceite from Marmolejo?
A: The Vía Verde del Aceite, a 120-kilometer car-free trail following a former railway line, is a significant cycling attraction in the broader region. While Marmolejo is not directly on the Vía Verde, it is situated at the foot of the Sierra Morena, providing access to routes that can connect you to this unique greenway. The Vía Verde itself is known for its gentle gradients, olive groves, old tunnels, and impressive steel viaducts, making it suitable for a wide range of cyclists.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes in Marmolejo?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' road cycling routes are not explicitly categorized, the region's diverse terrain includes options with gentle gradients, which can be suitable for families. Routes along the Guadalquivir River or those with less elevation gain would be good choices. For a truly car-free and gentle experience, consider sections of the nearby Vía Verde del Aceite, which is ideal for all ages.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Marmolejo?
A: The best time for road cycling in Marmolejo is generally during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, temperatures are milder and more comfortable for cycling, and the natural landscapes are particularly vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable, while winters are generally mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Marmolejo?
A: Yes, Marmolejo offers several excellent circular road cycling routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996080805/marmolejo-and-yeguas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marmolejo and Yeguas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 38.8 km trail. Another great choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31469199" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lopera Castle – Castillo de Lopera loop from Marmolejo&lt;/a&gt;, a 64.8 km route that takes you through historic areas.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Marmolejo?
A: The road cycling routes around Marmolejo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, which includes scenic olive groves, riverside paths along the Guadalquivir, and challenging ascents into the Sierra Morena foothills. The opportunity to explore natural attractions like the Martín Gonzalo Reservoir and enjoy extensive views is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. For experienced road cyclists seeking a challenge, Marmolejo offers several difficult routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123788" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Incredible views – Cardeña Town Center loop from Marmolejo&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 95.8 km path with significant elevation gain, providing extensive views and a rewarding experience.

Q: Where can I find scenic viewpoints along road cycling routes in Marmolejo?
A: Many routes around Marmolejo offer scenic viewpoints, especially those that climb into the Sierra Morena foothills or loop around reservoirs. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40299699" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Empire Viewpoint – Andújar loop from Marmolejo&lt;/a&gt; is designed to showcase impressive vistas. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5061639" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valley Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is a specific highlight where you can pause and enjoy the panoramic scenery.

Q: Are there opportunities to combine cycling with exploring historical sites?
A: Yes, road cycling routes in Marmolejo can easily be combined with visits to historical sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31469199" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lopera Castle – Castillo de Lopera loop from Marmolejo&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the historic area around Lopera Castle. You can also find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301171" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marmolejo Spa&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1279995" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Cabeza&lt;/a&gt;, which are significant cultural landmarks in the region.
tips: Historical bridge that crosses the Guadalquivir River, in the town of Castro del Rio, before beginning the ascent towards the Cardeña natural park.

The Yeguas reservoir is located between the provinces of Jaén and Córdoba. It collects the rainwater that falls on the Sierra de Cardeña, the natural park of Montoro and the Sierra de Andújar in the course of the Yeguas river.

The Spa of Marmolejo is located along the Guadalquivir river situated at the foot of the Sierra Morena mountains. Popular tourist attraction. From the bridge of the A-420 highway you can see the glass dome of the spa and the river.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: This is a moderate road cycling route, primarily on paved surfaces. However, be aware that some sections may be unpaved or present challenging conditions for a racebike. It includes a steady climb, especially at the beginning, offering rewarding views.

faqs.question: What level of fitness is required for this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, a good level of fitness is recommended. While the route is 38.8 kilometers long with an elevation gain of 340 meters, the potential for unpaved sections means you'll benefit from being comfortable handling varied conditions on a racebike.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable spots, including the Bridge over the Guadalquivir River in Castro del Río, the scenic Yeguas Reservoir, and the historical Marmolejo Spa. The route also offers panoramic views of the Sierra Morena and the reservoir.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The route winds through the beautiful Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar, offering picturesque views of Mediterranean forest and dehesa landscapes. You'll cycle along the tranquil waters of the Yeguas Reservoir, which forms a natural boundary between the provinces of Jaén and Córdoba. Spring is particularly vibrant with colors and scents.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Spring is an excellent time to cycle this route, as the Mediterranean forest and dehesa landscapes are especially vibrant with colors and scents during the flowering season. The weather is generally pleasant for cycling during this period.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public routes within the Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar. However, always respect local regulations and signage, especially concerning protected areas or private land.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily for racebikes, if you plan to bring a dog, it's important to note that dogs are generally allowed in the Parque Natural Sierra de Andújar but must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure their safety, especially in areas with livestock or other animals.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Specific parking details are not provided, but starting points like Villa del Río or areas near the Embalse del Yeguas would typically have parking options. It's advisable to check local parking availability in these towns before your trip.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Transandalus, Sendero de Sierra Morena, Barranco de La Palma, Polo, Ribera Guadalquivir, V P Vereda de Villa del Río al Empalme, La Campana, Thales, Camino de Enmedio, Pascal, GR48-Sierra Morena, Sendero del Balneario, VP Cordel de Recoche, Carril Colorao, La Aragonesa, Camino Corcomé, and Camino de la Zarzuela.

faqs.question: Is this a suitable route for families?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as moderate and includes sections that may be unpaved or challenging for racebikes. While scenic, its length (38.8 km) and varied terrain might be more suitable for families with older, experienced cyclists rather than young children or beginners.

faqs.question: What should I consider bringing for this cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and potential for varied terrain, it's wise to bring appropriate racebike gear, including a repair kit, sufficient water, and snacks. Sun protection is also recommended, especially during warmer months. Consider layers of clothing as temperatures can vary.

waypoints.name: Bridge over the Guadalquivir River in Castro del Río
